title: GDP and National Accounts
summary: Gross Domestic Product (GDP): three measurement approaches, nominal vs real GDP, GDP deflator, CPI, limitations of GDP as a welfare measure.
content_html:
GDP and National Accounts

GDP is the total market value of all final goods and services produced in an economy during a period of time. It is the primary measure of economic activity.

Why We Study GDP

  1. To assess and measure the performance of the economy.
  2. To gauge the health of the economy.
  3. To uncover underlying problems.
  4. To plot the long-run course of the economy over time.
  5. To provide a foundation for public policies.

Three Approaches to Measuring GDP

  • Expenditure Approach: GDP = C + I + G + (X - M)
    C = household consumption, I = private investment, G = government consumption, X = exports, M = imports.
  • Income Approach: Sums all incomes earned by factors of production: compensation of employees (wages) + gross operating surpluses (profits) + indirect taxes less subsidies.
  • Production (Value Added) Approach: Sums the value added at each stage of production across all industries. Avoids double-counting by only counting value added, not total sales.

Nominal vs Real GDP

  • Nominal GDP: Measured at current prices. Increases with both real output growth AND inflation.
  • Real GDP: Adjusted for inflation using a base year. Allows comparison of actual output over time.

Price Indices

  • Consumer Price Index (CPI): Measures the price level of a market basket of goods and services for a typical family. Published quarterly by the ABS.
  • GDP Deflator (Implicit Price Deflator): Measures average price changes across all GDP components (C, I, G, Net Exports). Broader than CPI.
  • Inflation rate: % change in CPI from one period to the next.

Shortcomings of GDP as a Welfare Measure

  • Does not capture distribution of income (inequality).
  • Does not include value of leisure.
  • Does not measure quality of health care.
  • Not adjusted for pollution or negative environmental effects.
  • Does not capture improvements in product quality.
  • Does not reflect changes in crime or social problems.

exercises.title: GDP definition
options: The total value of all goods produced in an economy
The total market value of all FINAL goods and services produced in an economy during a period of time
The total income earned by households only
The sum of all imports and exports
exercises.answer: The total market value of all FINAL goods and services produced in an economy during a period of time

exercises.title: Expenditure approach
exercises.prompt: The expenditure approach to calculating GDP uses the formula:
options: GDP = Wages + Profits + Taxes
GDP = C + I + G + (X - M)
GDP = Total Sales - Intermediate Goods
GDP = National Income + Depreciation

exercises.title: Value added approach
exercises.prompt: The value-added approach avoids double-counting by:
options: Only counting exports
Only counting final goods sold to consumers
Summing the value added at each stage of production
Multiplying output by the price level
exercises.answer: Summing the value added at each stage of production

exercises.title: Real vs nominal GDP
exercises.prompt: Real GDP differs from nominal GDP because real GDP:
options: Is always larger than nominal GDP
Is adjusted for inflation, allowing comparison of actual output over time
Only measures private sector output
Includes the underground economy
exercises.answer: Is adjusted for inflation, allowing comparison of actual output over time

exercises.title: CPI purpose
options: Total economic output of a country
The price level of a market basket of goods for a typical household
The growth rate of real GDP
The profit levels of Australian companies
exercises.answer: The price level of a market basket of goods for a typical household

exercises.title: GDP limitation
exercises.prompt: A key limitation of GDP as a measure of wellbeing is that it:
options: Is too difficult to calculate accurately
Does not capture the distribution of income or inequality
Measures too many goods and services
Overestimates the value of services
exercises.answer: Does not capture the distribution of income or inequality

exercises.title: Government expenditure in GDP
exercises.prompt: In the GDP expenditure formula (C + I + G + NX), 'G' represents:
options: Gross profit of firms
Government consumption and investment expenditure
Growth rate of the economy
Gold and commodity exports

exercises.title: Inflation calculation
exercises.prompt: The inflation rate is calculated as:
options: The absolute change in GDP from year to year
The percentage change in the CPI from one period to the next
Total money supply growth rate
The ratio of nominal to real GDP
exercises.answer: The percentage change in the CPI from one period to the next
